title: Meera — Making an AI assistant feel trustworthy
summary: Designing conversation, confidence, and correction for a financial AI assistant serving 4M+ users.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Meera was CRED's first user-facing AI product. It had to answer real money questions — 'why did my payment fail' — with humility, precision, and an obvious way out when it was wrong.

sections.heading: Problem
sections.body: Early prototypes were fluent but overconfident. Users treated Meera's guesses as facts, then blamed the product when a guess was wrong. Trust, once broken, was expensive to rebuild.

sections.heading: Research
sections.body: Diary studies with 22 users showed that confidence is a visual language: hedging phrases, source citations, and 'I don't know' options changed how people used the tool. I designed a confidence scale that mapped model uncertainty to explicit UI states.

sections.heading: Approach
sections.body: Every Meera response now shows one of three states: answer with source, best guess with 'verify' CTA, or a graceful handoff to a human. Micro-interactions — the way a suggestion settles, the way a correction is acknowledged — became part of the trust contract.

sections.heading: Outcome
sections.body: Task success rose 27% and support escalations dropped by nearly a third, even as usage grew. Users started forgiving mistakes because the product had already told them mistakes were possible.

sections.heading: Reflection
sections.body: AI design is less about surface polish and more about the honesty of the interaction. What the model says matters less than how the product carries what it doesn't know.
